abstract: This article examines the exposition of the first commandment within the Heidelberg Catechism (HC), providing a reconstruction of the original 16th-century German and Latin texts. As the primary architect of the HC, Zacharias Ursinus drew significant influence from his mentor in Wittenberg, Philipp Melanchthon. By revisiting two of Melanchthon’s seminal publications, this study uncovers the theological framework and intellectual context that shaped the Catechism’s development. Furthermore, the article analyzes Ursinus’s own detailed treatments of the first commandment found in his *Smaller* and *Larger Catechisms*, as well as insights preserved in his dogmatic lectures. Through this comparative analysis, the research offers a deeper understanding of the HC’s original intent and its enduring theological landscape.
